info.topictitle: 2.2.5 Movements along and shifts of the supply curve

markdown:
In the previous section we discussed how **price** and **non-price determinants of supply** have different effects on the supply curve:

1. **Price** causes **movements** along the curve
2. **Non-price determinants** causes **shifts **of the curve

### Movements


A **movement along the supply curve **occurs when the price of the good or service changes, and this change affects the **quantity supplied**.

![Figure 1: movements along the supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5f6671cf7e337bd6b9dcffb6c0052503f0676ef5-2314x1666.png)

The figure above shows the supply curve of Firm currently producing $Q_1$ of some good $X$ a price of $P_1$.

1. If the price rises to $P_2$, the **quantity supplied** increases to $Q_2$, resulting in an **expansion of supply**. 
2. This causes a movement along the curve from the point $A$ to the point $B$.
3. Conversely, if the price falls to $P_3$, the **quantity supplied** decreases to $Q_3$, resulting in a **contraction of supply**.
4. This causes a movement along the curve from the point $A$ to the point $C$.

<callout type="note">**Movements** on the curve are caused only by the** change in the price** of the good or service** itself**.</callout>

### Shifts


A **shift of the supply curve**, on the other hand, occurs when a **non-price determinant of supply **changes, altering the supply of the good or service at every price level.

<callout type="note">These **non-price determinants** are discussed thoroughly in the previous section (2.2.4)</callout>

![Figure 2: shifts of the supply curve](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/30ca457832f24ee84e52d517be7222e94aeaa4a7-2312x1786.png)

The figure above shows the supply curves of a Firm. **Initially** the supply curve of the firm is $S_1$ and it operates at a price $P_1$ with quantity $Q_3$.

1. If the supply of the firm** increases** _(due to any non-price determinant)_ for the same **given price**, then the **curve shifts to the right**.
2. This can be seen from the shift of $S_1$ to $S_2$ where the new point $B$ has the same price $P_1$ but a higher quantity $Q_2$.
3. Similarly, if the supply of the firm **decreases** for the same given price, then the curve **shifts to the left**.
4. This can be seen from the shift of $S_1$ to $S_3$ where the new point $C$ has the same price $P_1$ but a lower quantity $Q_3$.

<callout type="example">**Non-price determinants** are discussed in (2.2.4) already. Consider a good:

1. If the **cost** of production **falls**, **subsidies** received by the firm **increase**, a good in **joint supply** has a **higher price** (etc.).
2. As a result, the supply of the good increases causing a rightward shift.

The contrary happens too:

1. If the **cost** of production **increases**, **taxes increase**, a good in **competitive **supply has **higher price** (etc.).
2. then the supply of the good decreases causing a leftward shift.</callout>

<callout type="warning">Students often confuse **movements along the supply curve **with **shifts of the supply curve**.

Remember:

> **Price changes cause movements along the curve, while non-price determinants cause the curve to shift.**</callout>
